Excuses, excuses: No data? No dice

Jo Craven NICAR Staff November 1996
Government & Politics

Government agencies employ various tactics to deny journalists' data requests, from claiming laws are unimportant to arguing that compiling information is too resource-intensive, highlighting the challenges of accessing information in the public interest.

A variety of excuses are voiced by government agencies for denying data requests, ranging from trivial reasons to more elaborate bureaucratic hurdles. Journalists share anecdotes of absurd refusals, such as the Nevada county clerk dismissing a law as 'not important' and agencies claiming database redactions as creating new records. Some agencies state that compiling requested data is too resource-intensive or outright deny the existence of the data requested.

These experiences highlight the tiring negotiations and resourcefulness required to extract information critical for public interest.
